data.title: LLD — Revision Log
data.description: Every subtopic from your Low-Level Design guide — OOP fundamentals, SOLID, UML, all 23 GoF patterns, and full case studies — tracked the same way as your HLD and DevOps logs. Press Revised on a topic each time you go over it. Click into any subtopic for a deeper note and a reference link.

subs.d: A class is a logical blueprint bundling properties and behaviors; an object is a concrete memory allocation created from that blueprint at runtime. LLD interviews are really asking whether your objects hold real invariants, not just act as data bags.

Objects live on the heap and are garbage-collected; local primitive variables and object references live on the stack, which is faster to allocate/deallocate but scoped tightly to the current call frame. This distinction matters for reasoning about whether a "copy" duplicates data or just a reference.
`public`/`private`/`protected`/package-private restrict who can see or touch a member — the primary tool for enforcing a class's invariants at compile time.
Hiding state behind explicit getters/setters lets a class validate or transform values on the way in/out, instead of exposing raw mutable fields that any caller can corrupt.
A default constructor sets safe baseline state; a parameterized one requires the caller to supply required fields up front, catching invalid states at construction time instead of later.
Builds a new object from an existing one's state — getting this right matters a lot when the class holds references to mutable nested objects (see shallow vs deep copy).

Languages with deterministic cleanup (C++ destructors, `try-with-resources`/`AutoCloseable`, `IDisposable`) let you release file handles or sockets exactly when a scope ends. In GC'd languages like Java, `finalize()` exists but is unreliable and effectively deprecated — prefer an explicit `close()` method.

A shallow copy duplicates the top-level object but keeps references to the same nested objects, so mutating a nested object through the copy also affects the original. A deep copy recursively duplicates the entire object graph, giving true independence.

The classic bug: a class holds a `List` field, you shallow-clone it, and modifying the clone's list silently mutates the original's list too — always check whether a class's fields are themselves mutable before choosing a copy strategy.

Single-level is one parent, one child; multi-level is a chain (A→B→C); hierarchical is one parent with many children. Each models a different code-reuse relationship — a common LLD trap is reaching for inheritance when composition actually fits better ("favor composition over inheritance").
When a class would inherit conflicting implementations from two parents that both extend a common ancestor, it's ambiguous which implementation wins — the classic "diamond problem."
Java disallows multiple class inheritance entirely and instead uses interfaces; even interface default methods that conflict require the implementing class to explicitly resolve the ambiguity.

Method overriding resolves at runtime based on the object's actual type (vtable/dynamic dispatch) — this is what lets a `Shape` reference correctly call `Circle.draw()` or `Square.draw()` depending on what it actually points to.
Method overloading resolves at compile time based on argument types/count — same method name, different signature, decided before the program even runs.

Use an abstract class when subclasses share a core identity plus some common state/implementation ("is fundamentally a"); use an interface to define a pure contract of behavior that unrelated classes can all implement ("can do"). Many LLD answers hinge on getting this choice right.
Static members belong to the class itself, not any individual instance, and are shared across all objects — useful for counters, factory helpers, or true constants.
Lets a subclass explicitly invoke its parent's constructor or an overridden method, which matters whenever you want to extend rather than fully replace inherited behavior.
A class should have exactly one reason to change — mixing business logic with persistence or logging inside the same class means a change to either forces you to touch and re-test the whole class.

subs.t: Open for extension, closed for modification
subs.d: Classes should be extendable — typically via polymorphism and new subclasses — without editing existing, already-tested code, which prevents regression bugs. This is the core mechanism behind Strategy and most creational patterns.

Any subclass instance should be substitutable for its parent type without breaking correctness. A subclass that throws on a method the parent guaranteed to work, or narrows a precondition, violates LSP even if it compiles fine — the classic example is `Square extends Rectangle` breaking when `setWidth`/`setHeight` are called independently.

Prefer several small interfaces over one bloated general-purpose interface that forces implementers to stub out methods they don't need. A red flag is an interface method whose only real implementation is `throw new UnsupportedOperationException()`.

High-level business logic modules should depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete low-level classes. This is what makes a class testable (swap in a mock) and lets you change a database or third-party integration without touching business logic.
A general, often bi-directional relationship between two independent classes with no ownership implied — e.g. a Passenger rides a Cab; both exist and change independently of each other.
A "has-a" relationship where the child object can exist independently of the parent container — e.g. a Department has Professors, but a Professor still exists if the Department is dissolved.
A stricter "has-a" relationship where the child cannot exist without the parent — e.g. a Room cannot exist without its Building; deleting the Building deletes its Rooms too.
A parent-child relationship where a subclass extends a superclass's properties and methods — UML draws this with a solid line and a hollow triangle arrowhead.
A relationship where a concrete class implements the behaviors declared by an interface — drawn with a dashed line and hollow arrowhead, distinct from generalization's solid line.

Checking the null condition twice — once outside and once inside a synchronized block — avoids paying a lock's cost on every call while still preventing two threads from both constructing the instance concurrently.

The instance field must be declared `volatile`, or JVM/compiler instruction reordering can hand another thread a reference to a half-constructed object — a subtle bug interviewers specifically probe for.

A lock/mutex grants exclusive access to a critical section; a semaphore allows a bounded number of concurrent holders (e.g. "max 3 threads at once"); a monitor (Java's `synchronized`) pairs a lock with built-in wait/notify signaling. Knowing which fits a given LLD problem — like an elevator's shared request queue — is a common follow-up question.

An object whose state can never change after construction is inherently thread-safe with zero synchronization overhead. A large fraction of "how do you make this thread-safe" LLD questions are best answered by making the type immutable, not by bolting on locks.

A blocking queue lets producer and consumer threads coordinate safely without manual locking — the standard building block behind things like an order-matching engine's incoming request queue or a background job processor.
Ensures a class has exactly one instance globally with a single access point — classic uses include a config manager or a shared connection pool.
A singleton is effectively global mutable state, which makes unit testing harder; many teams prefer dependency injection over singletons for exactly that reason.

A dedicated creator class/method picks which concrete class to instantiate based on an input parameter, hiding `new` calls from client code — usually the first step before reaching for a full Factory Method.

Defines a creation method in a base class, but lets subclasses override it to decide which concrete class to instantiate — e.g. a `Dialog` base class with a `createButton()` method overridden per platform.

Provides an interface for creating families of related objects without naming their concrete classes — e.g. a `UIFactory` producing a matching Button+Checkbox+Scrollbar set per theme, guaranteeing the family stays visually consistent.

Separates constructing a complex object from representing it, letting you assemble it step by step and often producing an immutable final object — the standard answer whenever a constructor would otherwise need many optional parameters.

Creates new objects by cloning an existing instance rather than building from scratch — useful when construction is expensive (a DB round trip, heavy computation) and a close variant of an existing object is all you need.

Translates one class's interface into the interface a client expects, letting incompatible code work together without modifying either side — e.g. wrapping a legacy payment SDK behind your app's standard `PaymentGateway` interface.
Wraps an object to add behavior dynamically without touching its class — e.g. layering `CompressedStream(EncryptedStream(FileStream))`, with each decorator adding one capability.
Provides one unified interface in front of a complex subsystem — e.g. a single `OrderService.placeOrder()` call that internally coordinates inventory, payment, and shipping subsystems.
A stand-in for another object that controls access — used for lazy-loading expensive resources, enforcing access checks, or transparently adding logging/caching in front of the real object.

Splits an abstraction from its implementation so each can vary independently — e.g. separating "Shape" (Circle, Square) from "Renderer" (Vector, Raster) so you can mix and match without a subclass explosion.

Composes objects into tree structures representing part-whole hierarchies, letting client code treat a single leaf and a whole subtree uniformly — the natural fit for a file-system model or a UI component tree.

subs.t: Sharing common state to save memory
subs.d: Shares common, immutable state across many objects instead of duplicating it, drastically cutting memory when you need huge numbers of similar objects — e.g. sharing glyph/font data across every character instance in a text editor.
Establishes a one-to-many dependency where observers get notified automatically whenever the subject's state changes — the backbone of event listeners, pub-sub UIs, and reactive notification systems.
Wraps a request as an object, so you can queue it, log it, parameterize callers with different requests, and support undo/redo by keeping a history of executed command objects.

Encapsulates a family of interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface and lets the caller swap which is active at runtime — e.g. swapping payment strategies (CreditCard, UPI, Wallet) without changing the checkout flow.

Defines the fixed skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, deferring specific steps to subclasses — e.g. a base `DataParser` fixing the "open, read, parse, close" sequence while subclasses implement `parse()` per file format.
Lets an object change its behavior when its internal state changes, by delegating to a State object — the elevator's IDLE/MOVING_UP/MOVING_DOWN/MAINTENANCE modeling is the textbook example.
Passes a request along a chain of handlers, each deciding whether to process it or forward it further — e.g. a support-ticket system escalating from L1 to L2 to L3 until someone handles it.
Provides a way to access an aggregate's elements sequentially without exposing its underlying representation — what powers `for-each` loops over custom collection types.

Centralizes how a set of objects communicate, so they refer to the mediator instead of each other directly — reduces a tangled many-to-many web of references into a single hub, e.g. an air-traffic-control tower coordinating planes that never talk to each other directly.
Captures and externalizes an object's internal state so it can be restored later, without violating encapsulation — the standard mechanism behind undo history in an editor.

Lets you add new operations to a class hierarchy without modifying the classes themselves, by having each class accept a visitor that implements the new operation per concrete type — useful when operations change often but the object structure is stable, the mirror-image situation from Strategy.

The SCAN/LOOK algorithm services all requests in one direction before reversing, avoiding starvation and minimizing unnecessary direction changes — the standard answer for "how does the elevator decide which floor to go to next."

ElevatorController coordinates multiple ElevatorCar instances; each car has a ButtonPanel (Internal/External buttons), a Gate, and a Direction enum — getting this decomposition right is most of the interview.
The State pattern models IDLE, MOVING_UP, MOVING_DOWN, and MAINTENANCE cleanly, letting each state control its own valid transitions instead of a tangle of if/else.
An 8×8 grid of Spot objects, each optionally holding a Piece subclass (King, Queen, Knight, etc.) — favor polymorphic `piece.getValidMoves(board)` over one giant switch statement.

Move legality (check, checkmate, castling, en passant) is naturally modeled with the Strategy pattern — one validation strategy per piece type, composed for full-board rules like "does this move leave my own king in check."
A Game object tracks the active Player, move history (useful for undo and detecting draws by repetition), and any Spectators observing the match.
Multiple floors with different spot sizes (compact/large/handicapped); a `ParkingSpot` subclass hierarchy plus a strategy for finding the nearest available matching spot is the typical design core.
A `Ticket` records entry time and assigned spot; a pluggable pricing Strategy (hourly, flat-rate, per-vehicle-type) computes the fee at exit, keeping pricing changes isolated from the core flow.
Multiple entry gates allocating spots concurrently need thread-safe spot reservation (synchronized allocation or an atomic compare-and-swap on spot status) to avoid double-assigning the same spot.

A stateful, transactional booking engine handles many users selecting the same seat in parallel — typically a short-lived hold on the seat during payment, released on timeout if payment doesn't complete.
Atomic inventory updates prevent double-booking; the Transactional Outbox Pattern keeps "seat booked" DB writes and downstream ticketing events consistent without a fragile distributed transaction.
User, Train, Schedule, Station, Coach, Seat, Booking, PaymentTransaction — note that Schedule (not Train) is usually what seats actually attach to, since one Train runs many Schedules over time.
A segment tree or interval tree structure detects overlapping bookings faster than a linear scan, which matters once a room has hundreds of bookings to check against.
Availability queries filter by capacity and facilities (projector, video conferencing) in addition to the time-interval check.
Decoupling the act of scheduling from sending emails/reminders via an Observer (or event-driven pub-sub) keeps the booking flow fast and notification logic independently extensible.
Equal, exact-amount, and percentage splits are naturally modeled as a Strategy hierarchy behind a common `SplitStrategy.getSplits(amount, participants)` interface.

After many expenses, users owe each other a tangle of small amounts; a "debt simplification" step nets these down to the minimum number of settling transactions — a classic min-cash-flow graph problem.
User, Group, Expense, Split, and a Balance/Ledger per user-pair — most designs keep a running balance sheet rather than recomputing from the full expense history on every query.

modules.title: Case Studies III: Real-Time & Domain Systems

subs.d: A hash map (O(1) key lookup) paired with a doubly linked list (O(1) move-to-front and O(1) tail eviction) is the standard implementation — a very common "write it live" LLD/coding hybrid question.
A production LRU cache needs a lock (or a concurrent-friendly structure) around both the map and list mutations, since `get()` also mutates recency order — a subtlety interviewers often probe.
Token Bucket allows short bursts up to a cap, refilling steadily; sliding-window counters give smoother, more precise limiting at slightly higher bookkeeping cost — be ready to compare them directly.
A single-server in-memory limiter is easy, but a real distributed rate limiter needs shared state (Redis with atomic INCR/EXPIRE) so limits hold across all app server instances, not per-instance.

An in-memory limit order book matches buy/sell orders (typically price-time priority) with sub-millisecond execution — usually modeled with sorted price-level structures rather than a naive list scan.

A trade copier replicates a master account's orders across many child accounts in parallel — needs careful handling of partial fills and per-child position limits so one child's failure doesn't block the others.

Normalizes incoming binary exchange feeds into clean JSON/Protobuf objects before pushing to client WebSockets — keeping this normalization layer separate is what lets the rest of the system stay exchange-agnostic.

A search engine filtering listings by rent budget, location radius, amenities, and layout — typically modeled as a composable set of filter/Specification objects that combine (a Strategy-pattern variant).

Nearby-listing queries use spatial points (lat/long) with a geospatial index for the coarse radius filter, then compute exact walking distance only for the shortlisted candidates to keep the expensive calculation bounded.
